attributes.stem:
A four-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with her parent with a three-day history of a bright red facial rash and body. She had a mild fever and cold-like symptoms one week ago which resolved. She attends daycare three days a week and was absent when she was unwell last week. She has coeliac disease and takes no regular medications. Her immunisations are up to date.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is an erythematous rash on both cheeks. A lace-like rash is present on her trunk and limbs. One of the daycare workers is 16 weeks pregnant.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Isolation from daycare for two weeks
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of erythema infectiosum (fifth disease), caused by Parvovirus B19. The illness typically begins with non-specific viral symptoms of fever and fatigue in which the patient is most infectious, followed by the characteristic 'slapped cheek' appearance for 1 - 3 days, and then a maculopapular rash which evolves to a lace-like rash on the trunk and limbs. The reticular rash may continue intermittently for months with exposure to heat. Rarely it can cause arthralgia with adults, chronic bone marrow failure if the patient is immunodeficient, or a transient aplastic crisis in patients with haemolytic anaemia.

No specific intervention is required as parvovirus B19 infection is typically self-limiting in immunocompetent children. By the time the characteristic rash appears, the child is no longer infectious and thus does not need to be isolated.

Isolation from daycare is unnecessary as the patient is no longer infectious when the rash appears. The period of infectivity occurs during the prodromal phase, before the development of the characteristic rash.

Oral acyclovir would not be effective as this is an antiviral medication specific for herpes virus infections, not parvovirus.

Topical corticosteroids are not indicated as the rash is not primarily inflammatory and will resolve without intervention. Symptoms are typically mild in immunocompetent children and management is supportive only.

Oral antibiotics would not be appropriate as this is a viral infection that does not require antimicrobial therapy.

The pregnant daycare worker should be referred for serological testing as parvovirus B19 infection during pregnancy of less than 20 weeks can rarely lead to miscarriage or hydrops fetalis in 50 % of cases. If the worker is found to be non-immune and has been exposed, close monitoring of the pregnancy would be required.
learningPoints: Parvovirus B19 infection (fifth disease) is typically self-limiting and requires only supportive management in immunocompetent children.
The characteristic 'slapped cheek' rash appears after the period of infectivity has passed.
references: McCarter-Spaulding D, Parovirus B19 in Pregnancy. Journal of Obstetric, Gynaecologic and Neonatal Nursing. 2002: 31 (1).  Available at: https://www.jognn.org/article/S0884-2175(15)33944-7/fulltext
Frydenberg A, Starr M. Slapped Cheek Disease. Australian Family Physician. 2003L 32 (8).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/781cab1a-fe58-499f-9041-7563c7638933/attachment.aspx

NSW Health. Parvovirus B19 and Fifth Disease [Internet]. NSW Government; [cited 2025 Jul 27]. Available from: https://www.health.nsw.gov.au/Infectious/factsheets/Pages/parvovirus-B19-and-(fifth-disease).aspx

The Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne. Slapped cheek (fifth disease) [Internet]. Kids Health Info. [updated unspecified date; accessed 2025 Jul 27]. Available from: https://www.rch.org.au/kidsinfo/fact_sheets/Slapped_cheek_fifth_disease/
